repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Problem:
`nvim_create_autocmd` is too verbose and its `callback` requires extra "nesting".

Solution:
Introduce `nvim_on`. Start using it internally. Then we can get a feel for how it should look before making it public.

comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -172,4 +172,31 @@ function M.echo_err(msg)
   vim.api.nvim_echo({ { msg } }, true, { err = true })
 end
 
+--- Define event-handlers (autocmds) ergonomically.
+---
+--- Examples:
+--- ```lua
+---   local nvim_on = require('vim._core.util').nvim_on
+---   nvim_on('BufWritePost', group, function(ev) print(ev.file) end)
+---   nvim_on({ 'BufRead', 'BufNew' }, group, { pattern = '*.lua' }, function(ev) end)
+---   nvim_on('VimLeavePre', nil, function() end)
+--- ```
+---
+--- @param events string|string[] Event(s) to watch. See |autocmd-events|.
+--- @param group string|integer? Augroup name or id; `nil` for no group.
+--- @param opts_or_fn vim.api.keyset.create_autocmd|fun(ev) Options table (followed by {fn}), or the callback for the no-opts shorthand.
+--- @param fn? fun(ev) Callback. Required when {opts_or_fn} is a table.
+--- @return integer # Autocmd id (see |nvim_create_autocmd()|).
+function M.nvim_on(events, group, opts_or_fn, fn)

comment_data.body:
See https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/19156

"autocmd" in general is not a useful concept, it's a quirky name for "event handlers".

"Augroups" will be merged with "namespaces": https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/28441

And besides, `vim.on` is a shorter and more readable.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -172,4 +172,31 @@ function M.echo_err(msg)
   vim.api.nvim_echo({ { msg } }, true, { err = true })
 end
 
+--- Define event-handlers (autocmds) ergonomically.
+---
+--- Examples:
+--- ```lua
+---   local nvim_on = require('vim._core.util').nvim_on
+---   nvim_on('BufWritePost', group, function(ev) print(ev.file) end)
+---   nvim_on({ 'BufRead', 'BufNew' }, group, { pattern = '*.lua' }, function(ev) end)
+---   nvim_on('VimLeavePre', nil, function() end)
+--- ```
+---
+--- @param events string|string[] Event(s) to watch. See |autocmd-events|.
+--- @param group string|integer? Augroup name or id; `nil` for no group.
+--- @param opts_or_fn vim.api.keyset.create_autocmd|fun(ev) Options table (followed by {fn}), or the callback for the no-opts shorthand.
+--- @param fn? fun(ev) Callback. Required when {opts_or_fn} is a table.
+--- @return integer # Autocmd id (see |nvim_create_autocmd()|).
+function M.nvim_on(events, group, opts_or_fn, fn)

comment_data.body: should this be a hard assert that (if not a function, thon) `opts_or_fn` is a table, or do we want to allow `nvim_on(event, group, nil, callback)`  ?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -172,4 +172,31 @@ function M.echo_err(msg)
   vim.api.nvim_echo({ { msg } }, true, { err = true })
 end
 
+--- Define event-handlers (autocmds) ergonomically.
+---
+--- Examples:
+--- ```lua
+---   local nvim_on = require('vim._core.util').nvim_on
+---   nvim_on('BufWritePost', group, function(ev) print(ev.file) end)
+---   nvim_on({ 'BufRead', 'BufNew' }, group, { pattern = '*.lua' }, function(ev) end)
+---   nvim_on('VimLeavePre', nil, function() end)
+--- ```
+---
+--- @param events string|string[] Event(s) to watch. See |autocmd-events|.
+--- @param group string|integer? Augroup name or id; `nil` for no group.
+--- @param opts_or_fn vim.api.keyset.create_autocmd|fun(ev) Options table (followed by {fn}), or the callback for the no-opts shorthand.
+--- @param fn? fun(ev) Callback. Required when {opts_or_fn} is a table.
+--- @return integer # Autocmd id (see |nvim_create_autocmd()|).
+function M.nvim_on(events, group, opts_or_fn, fn)
+  local opts --- @type vim.api.keyset.create_autocmd
+  if type(opts_or_fn) == 'function' then
+    fn, opts = opts_or_fn, {}
+  else
+    opts = opts_or_fn or {}

comment_regular_data.body:
> Then we can get a feel for how it should look before making it public.

I hope early feedback is still welcome. I completely agree with both `` ... too verbose and its `callback` requires extra "nesting". `` That's how I've been seeing a more practical signature for a long time:

- `nvim_on(events, action, opts)`. The logic is that required arguments are first+explicit while non-required are in `opts`. Any autocommand (or "on" hook) requires exactly two parts: when to execute and what to execute. Everything else is supplementary.
- The `action` can be `function|string`. The `function` is equivalent to `opts.callback` and `string`is equivalent to `opts.command`.
- The `opts.group` can be string as a stand-in for `vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd(opts.group, { clear = false })`. This makes it less verbose.

> `nvim_on(events, action, opts)`. The logic is that required arguments are first+explicit while non-required are in `opts`.

I actually like the current way of `opts` before `action`, as `opts` are typically short (something like `{ buf = buf }` or `{ pattern = 'termguicolors' }` while `action` is typically some multi-line function. While I see the utility of having `opts` be an optional argument, I think I slightly prefer `opts` coming first. I don't feel that strongly about this though.

> The `opts.group` can be string as a stand-in for `vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd(opts.group, { clear = false })`. This makes it less verbose.

+1 to this, but I think this is another argument for `opts` coming before `action`.

> I actually like the current way of `opts` before `action`, as `opts` are typically short (something like `{ buf = buf }` or `{ pattern = 'termguicolors' }` while `action` is typically some multi-line function. While I see the utility of having `opts` be an optional argument, I think I slightly prefer `opts` coming first. I don't feel that strongly about this though.

Even excluding `callback`, the `opts` for autocommand is rarely *that* short in my experience. For a good behaving autocommand it usually has both `group` and `desc`. Slightly less frequent `pattern` / `buf`, while `nested` and `once` are not seldom also.

I think designing with objective to have a good formatting is rarely a good approach. Either adjusting formatting style or code style (like extract callback in a separate `local function cb()`) is more viable long term.

To be honest, the number of possible options is exactly what makes the current "keyword style" signature of the Nvim API feel good to me. (Couple that with the fact that I sometimes mix callbacks with ex commands, so the uniform API more than offsets the extra keyword for me.)

So for me -- and please take that as constructive feedback -- I don't see the value of the new function (yet) and will continue to use the current API (which luckily won't get deprecated 😌)

> and its `callback` requires extra "nesting".

just for my own knowledge: What is the extra nesting referring to and why do we consider it problematic?

> > and its `callback` requires extra "nesting".
> 
> 
> 
> just for my own knowledge: What is the extra nesting referring to and why do we consider it problematic?

I believe it's referring to `callback` being nested inside the `opts` of `nvim_create_autocmd` even if it is the only 'option'

> just for my own knowledge: What is the extra nesting referring to and why do we consider it problematic?

It requires an extra level of indentation.

> Even excluding `callback`, the `opts` for autocommand is rarely _that_ short in my experience. For a good behaving autocommand it usually has both `group` and `desc`. Slightly less frequent `pattern` / `buf`, while `nested` and `once` are not seldom also.

for plugins and public event-handlers, yes, i agree.

but `desc` is often not used for internal stuff, and furthermore, for users writing their config, creating event-handlers should be very ergonomic and should not involve a "reminder" that they "should" write a `desc`.

> designing with objective to have a good formatting is rarely a good approach

is "formatting" a synonym for "readability"? it matters very much because events are increasingly (and intended to be) a user-facing interface that often is an alternative to introducing new options. 

though it's true that not all cases are an improvement, this is a clear improvement:

- before
  ```lua
  vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd('FileType', {
    pattern = 'checkhealth',
    once = true,
    callback = function(ev)
    ...
  ```
- after:
  ```lua
  nvim_on('FileType', nil, { pattern = 'checkhealth', once = true }, function(ev)
    ...
  ```
- and i think this would be a further improvement (not in this PR, might explore later):
  ```lua
  nvim_on('FileType', nil, 'checkhealth', { once = true }, function(ev)
    ...
  ```

and btw, `nvim_on` can accept both the "kwargs" form and the non-kwargs form.

> > designing with objective to have a good formatting is rarely a good approach
> 
> is "formatting" a synonym for "readability"? it matters very much because events are increasingly (and intended to be) a user-facing interface that often is an alternative to introducing new options.

I meant "formatting" as in "decide the order of the arguments based on how the code is meant to be formatted". My assumption is the function is last because the following is bad:

```lua
nvim_on('FileType', function(ev)
  vim.treesitter.start(ev.buf)
end, { pattern = 'checkhealth', group = 'user' })
```

For short functions it might be autoformatted by StyLua as a single line. For other cases I'd much more recommend extracting into a local function, even for configs:

```lua
local start_ts = function(ev)
  vim.treesitter.start(ev.buf)
end
nvim_on('FileType', start_ts, { pattern = 'checkhealth', group = 'user' })
```

This is always only +1 line of code (which is not huge even as it scales in the most common use cases) and having to name the function improves readability.
function is always the last param. we settled that and documented it long ago, it's not up for debate again

It is awkward that `group` separates event and pattern here, and especially for `User` commands. Event and pattern together describe the condition to execute (even regarding the special case of `:doau`, there's little reason to put group between the other two). In the current API, one can at least put pattern as the first option, matching the `:autocommand` interface. If an optional argument should be promoted to a positional one, pattern might make a lot more sense.

Which leads to the elephant in the room, that is, `group` being, more often than not, an obscure boilerplate just to prevent double-sourcing, even for solo autocommands.

> Which leads to the elephant in the room, that is, `group` being, more often than not, an obscure boilerplate just to prevent double-sourcing, even for solo autocommands.

bingo. that's the kind of thing that I want to think about. some of these params that feel "necessary" may not be, maybe we can design around it.

part of the story here is https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/28441 and other ideas. this PR is just a baby step towards a slightly more ergonomic util, to get in the habit of using it. it will be reshaped as we discover the best patterns.

> It is awkward that `group` separates event and pattern here, and especially for `User` commands.

One thought I had to solve this is to make `group` an optional index-able parameter of `nvim_on` (and eventually `vim.on`), like so:

```lua
vim.on['nvim.editorconfig']('BufWritePre', { buf = bufnr }, function()
  local view = vim.fn.winsaveview()
  vim.api.nvim_command('silent! undojoin')
  vim.api.nvim_command('silent keepjumps keeppatterns %s/\\s\\+$//e')
  vim.fn.winrestview(view)
end)
```

This way, if you're defining a bunch of autocommands with the same group, you can do `local group = vim.on[group_name]` and call `group()` to create autocommands.

`vim.on()` would still be directly callable and would create an autocommand with no group.

My only worry with this syntax is that it seems "too magical", but I'm bringing it up because I think it looks very nice. Would love to hear others thoughts on this though.

Alternatively I do also like @echasnovski's idea of specifying a group as part of `opts`.
I put my thoughts on `vim.on` here: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/discussions/40320.

title: refactor: introduce nvim_on internally
